coredata.dc:title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield by a facile one-step method and applications for cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Jie
Zhang, Xin
Tong, Xiaolei
Wang, Xueyuan
Yang, Panxing
Yao, Fanglian
Guo, Ruiwei
Yuan, Caideng
coredata.dc:description: The preparation and biological applications of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention. Here, a one-step hydrothermal method for synthesizing nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) using graphene oxide (GO), ethylenediamine and hydrogen peroxide, simultaneously achieved oxidative cleavage and chemical reduction of GO. The average size of the synthesized N-GQDs was about 1.84 ± 0.28 nm and their quantum yield of the N-GQDs reached about 0.46, which was higher than that of other GQDs synthesized by top-down methods. The cytotoxicity of these N-GQDs on Hela cells was evaluated using a cell counting Kit-8 assay. The effects of N-GQDs on different cell lines and fluorescence imaging were also observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. The results suggested that these N-GQDs penetrated into cells by endocytosis and were promising fluorescent probes for biological imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Luminescence
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal reaction
Bio-imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-GQDs. Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution of N-GQDs. (b) AFM image of N-GQDs. Inset: Height profile of N-GQDs along the lines in (b). (c) AFM image of GO. Inset: Height profile of GO along the lines in (c). (d) XPS survey spectra of GO and N-GQDs. Well-fitted high-resolution (e) C-1s and (f) N-1s spectrum of N-GQDs.

(a) PL emission spectra of N-GQDs aqueous dispersion at different excitation wavelengths (350–430 nm). (b) UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of N-GQDs; inset, photograph of N-GQDs under daylight and 365 nm UV light. (c) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of N-GQDs.
(a) Cytotoxicity evaluation tests of Hela cells with different concentrations of N-GQDs after 24 h incubation. (b) CLSM images of control and Hela cells incubated with N-GQDs for 24 h.
Quantum yield of N-GQDs (excitation wavelength, 390 nm; emission wavelength range, 400–750 nm).

head.ce:title: Preparation of graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield by a facile one-step method and applications for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300350, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300350, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin University-UCGM Joint Research Center for Graphene Application, Tianjin 300350, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Research Center for Graphene Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin University-UCGM Joint Research Center for Graphene Application, Tianjin 300350, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation and biological applications of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention. Here, a one-step hydrothermal method for synthesizing nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) using graphene oxide (GO), ethylenediamine and hydrogen peroxide, simultaneously achieved oxidative cleavage and chemical reduction of GO. The average size of the synthesized N-GQDs was about 1.84 ± 0.28 nm and their quantum yield of the N-GQDs reached about 0.46, which was higher than that of other GQDs synthesized by top-down methods. The cytotoxicity of these N-GQDs on Hela cells was evaluated using a cell counting Kit-8 assay. The effects of N-GQDs on different cell lines and fluorescence imaging were also observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. The results suggested that these N-GQDs penetrated into cells by endocytosis and were promising fluorescent probes for biological imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new kind of carbon-based quantum dots, possess many novel chemical and physical propertied [1]. GQDs show intrinsic luminescence as a result of quantum confinement, surface defects, and edge effects, and their fluorescent properties can be changed by doping or controlling their size and morphology [2]. One of the major application areas for GQDs is in bio-imaging, with their intrinsic luminescence properties, lateral size, and biocompatibility being key features for biological applications.

One of the main two strategies for synthesizing GQDs is a top-down approach [1], which refers to the cleavage of large-sized carbon materials, such as graphite [3], graphene oxide (GO) [4], carbon nanotubes [5], carbon fibers [6] or carbon black [7]. As GQDs synthesized by top-down approaches have been reported to always show low quantum yields as well as difficulties in morphology control, the latter which hinders mass production. Therefore, here, a one-step hydrothermal method was considered for increasing the quantum yield while also enhancing the fluorescence performance through doping and reduction.

In this study, a facile, green, and efficient one-step hydrothermal route was developed for synthesizing N-GQDs using GO as precursor, ethylenediamine (EDA) as the nitrogen source and reducing agent, and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) for oxidative cutting. The N-GQDs had small lateral dimensions of 1.84 ± 0.28 nm average size and their fluorescence exhibited excitation-dependent properties. High quantum yield, long fluorescence lifetime, low cytotoxicity, and excellent ability for imaging yielded these N-GQDs good candidates for biological imaging.

Precursor GO was synthesized by a modified Hummers method from graphite powder [5]. To prepare N-GQDs, 30 mg of GO, 7.5 mL of EDA, and 2 mL of H2O2 (20 wt% in water) were dissolved in 30 mL of ultra-pure water. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL para-polyphenol autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting dark-brown product was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the black gelatinous substance. The obtained dispersion was dried in an oven to obtain the powder, which was then used in subsequent characterizations.

The N-GQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL Ltd., Japan), atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension Icon, Bruker Crop., USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA), UV Vis spectrometer (UV-3200, Mapada Instruments Co., Ltd., China) and fluorescence spectrophotometry (Varian Cary-Eclipse, Agilent Technologies, Inc., USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.). Hela cells were cultured with Dulbecco’s modified Eagles’ medium (DMEM) which included 10% fetal bovine serum and penicillin/streptomycin in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37℃. The cytotoxicity of N-GQDs was measured at 450 nm using an Epoch 2T reader (Bio Tek Instruments, Inc., USA) with a CCK-8 assay. Different cell lines were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Leica TCS SP8, Leica Microsystems GmbH, Germany).

To obtain optimal synthetic methods, multiple sets of experiments were performed (Fig. S1). From TEM images of the N-GQDs, they were uniformly distributed spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 1 a). They exhibited a relatively narrow size distribution, between 1.27 and 2.34 nm with an average lateral size of 1.84 ± 0.28 nm and matched a Gaussian distribution. A high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image indicated their high crystallinity and the lattice parameter of 0.24 nm represented the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene (Fig. 1a, upper inset) [4]. AFM images of N-GQDs and GO demonstrated that GO was oxidatively cut into nano-scale N-GQDs during hydrothermal processing (Fig. 1b and c). Also, the N-GQDs thicknesses were distributed around 1.3 nm, which was about 1–2 graphene layers [8]. Furthermore, XPS spectra, collected to explore the particles’ elemental composition, showed that, compared with GO, a pronounced N-1s peak at 400.0 eV was observed in N-GQDs (Fig. 1d). C-N (285.1 eV) bonds were observed in high resolution C-1s spectrum (Fig. 1e). And high-resolution N-1s spectra of N-GQDs demonstrated the existence of pyridine N (398.2 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.6 eV, Fig. 1f) [9]. FTIR spectra can also proved the existence of N (Fig. S2). In addition, the O/C atomic ratio of N-GQDs was 29.15% while in GO, was 52.48%, indicating that the N-GQDs had been reduced to a great extent [8,9]. These results demonstrated that EDA not only provided a N source for N-GQDs, but also reduced N-GQDs.

Ultraviolet visible light (UV–Vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured to explore the optical properties of these N-GQDs. When the excitation wavelength increased from 350 to 430 nm, the emission peak showed a red shift, indicating an excitation-dependent PL property (Fig. 2 a). This behavior was traced to the incorporation of O/N functional groups in N-GQDs [10]. The UV Vis absorption spectrum showed a shoulder peak at 202 nm and a broad peak at 340 nm (Fig. 2b, blue line). The pronounced absorption peak below 300 nm was attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic domains, and the broad peak at 340 nm assigned to n-π* transitions of multi-functional groups and sp2 domains [10]. The one-photon fluorescence emission and excitation spectra indicated that the maximum emission and excitation wavelengths were located at 390 and 466 nm (Fig. 2b, black and red lines, respectively). The N-GQDs aqueous dispersion emitted a bright blue fluorescence under irradiation with a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2b, inset). The fluorescence lifetime curve of these N-GQDs indicated that the average emission lifetime of N-GQDs was 7.20 ns (τ1 = 2.38 ns and, 39.98%; τ2 = 8.30 ns and, 60.02%; Fig. 2c) [11].

In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs was measured using a one-point method and calculated to be 0.46 using Rhodamine B as a reference (Table 1 ) [4], which was higher than previously reported GQDs synthesized by top-down methods (Table S1) [3–7]. The observed high quantum yield was attributed to N-atom doping and surface reduction of N-containing groups. Moreover, the product yield was about 20%, and the mechanism for synthesis can be found in supplementary information (Fig. S3).

Assessment of particle cytotoxicity was essential for possible biological applications. When the N-GQDs concentration increased from 1 to 100 μg·mL−1, cell viability remained >90% (Fig. 3 a). These results suggested that these N-GQDs exhibited low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility and were promising candidates as fluorescent probes [12]. The morphologies of cells incubated with N-GQDs were almost unchanged compared to control cells under bright field observations (Fig. 3b). CLSM results further confirmed that these N-GQDs showed low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Meanwhile, Hela cells incubated with N-GQDs clearly exhibited bright blue fluorescence under 405 nm excitation. This fluorescence was mainly concentrated in the cytoplasm and rarely observed in the nucleus, indicating that the small size of N-GQDs facilitated entry into cytoplasm by endocytosis without genetic disruption [12,13]. The cytotoxicity and CLSM imaging of N-GQDs in HepG2 and LO2 cells were also tested (Figs. S4 and S5). These results indicated that these N-GQDs had broad application prospects in bio-imaging research.

In summary, a facile and efficient hydrothermal method was developed to synthesize N-GQDs using GO. The prepared N-GQDs exhibited outstanding optical properties, including bright and stable blue fluorescence, high fluorescence QY reaching 0.46, and long fluorescence lifetime of 7.20 ns. The N-GQDs also possessed relatively great biocompatibility and excellent bio-imaging ability. All these properties yielded N-GQDs to be promising candidates for biological imaging, disease diagnosis, and bio-sensing.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare regarding the publication of this paper.

Jie Su: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Xin Zhang: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Xiaolei Tong: Data curation, Software. Xueyuan Wang: Investigation, Validation. Panxing Yang: Methodology, Validation. Fanglian Yao: Supervision, Resources. Ruiwei Guo: Supervision, Resources. Caideng Yuan: Project administration, Resources, Conceptualization, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.127806.
